INTRODUCTION : This document contains the resources required for organizing and maintaining the student’s clubs & chapters at the school of CS&AI. This compilation of materials and practices includes advice about recruiting members, ideas for activities, how to schedule for running meetings and achieve milestones and much more. Objective: To enable students’ participation in the development of the University. Their career, personality, and organizational skills through interactive programs. Composition: The Dean of the school of CS&AI will be the Chief Patron and Faculty coordinator/mentor be the Patron of the Students Club. Each club will have an Executive Committee and Representatives. Executive Committee School of CS&AI student clubs are required to have a Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary, Treasurer, Marketing & PR secretary and other possible operational roles who are the SoCS&AI students, as well as a Faculty Sponsor. The major criteria of Executive Committee will be as follows: Executive Committee 1. Chairperson: 2nd & 3rd Year Students 2. Vice-Chair: From 1st to 3rd year Students 3. Secretary: From 1st to 3rd year Students 4. Treasurer: From 1st to 3rd year Students 5. Marketing & PR Secretary: From 1st to 3rd year Students Operational Committee 6. Web Master: From 1st to 3rd year Students 7. Membership Chair: From 1st to 3rd year Students 8. Management Head: From 1st to 3rd year Students 9. Content & Creative Head: From 1st to 3rd year Students 10. Digital & Social Media Head: From 1st to 3rd year Students Please Note that two more additional Positions can be created by the individual Club/Chapter as per the need but no more than 12 members are allowed in the Core Committee (Executive --- PAGE 3 --- Team Level 1 and Operational Team Level 2). On record it must be submitted to the Office once the Committee/Club is formed. 11. Faculty Mentor must be from the School of Computer Science & AI 2. RESPONSIBILITIES OF CLUBS: Club office bearers are charged with meeting the needs of their members, activities and other responsibilities given by Dean. Clubs are responsible for maintaining the frequency and quality of activity in the area they serve, and for identifying and promoting those activities of specific interest

Operational Team Level 2). On record it must be submitted to the Office once the Committee/Club is formed. 11. Faculty Mentor must be from the School of Computer Science & AI 2. RESPONSIBILITIES OF CLUBS: Club office bearers are charged with meeting the needs of their members, activities and other responsibilities given by Dean. Clubs are responsible for maintaining the frequency and quality of activity in the area they serve, and for identifying and promoting those activities of specific interest to their members. Each club must communicate with the faculty mentor; submit annual financial and activity reports; report changes in officers and contact information; update club member lists, and management of funds. This section defined the duties and responsibilities of each club in addition to common responsibilities. 2.1 Duties and Key Responsibilities of The Core Committee of each club/chapter: 1. Hackathon Student Club: Faculty coordinator: Mr. Durgunala Ranjith 1 Measure the quantity and quality of hackathons organized by the club, including participation rates, feedback, and impact. 2 Track the number of students involved in club activities, their level of engagement, and satisfaction with the club's events. 3 Assess the growth of students' technical skills, problem-solving abilities, and teamwork through pre- and post-hackathon assessments. 4 Measure the number and quality of partnerships established with industry mentors, sponsors, and organizations. 5 Track the success of students in hackathons, including awards, recognition, and career opportunities. 6 Support and help to other Clubs and Students for conducting Hackathon and Participating in it. 10. Digital Design Media & Branding Club: Faculty coordinator: Mr. S. Deepan 1 Skill Development: Organize workshops, seminars, and guest lectures on digital media, branding, and marketing. RESPONSIBILITIES OF CLUB OFFICERS Each club will have a core committee, which includes elected club officers, the past Chairperson. The club’s core committee is generally responsible for managing the club’s affairs (except for electing officers and amending bylaws). It is essential for the core committee to be open to the suggestions and feedback of the club membership and should always be open to offers of help. Upon assuming the office, all core committee members should contact the faculty mentor to introduce him/herself, review club bylaws and policies and procedures, and review the events of the past year with his/her predecessors. The Club Chair The Chair is the principal officer and is responsible for leading the Club and managing its activities in accordance with the policies and procedures of School. The club Chairperson has the overall responsibility for developing club programs, for ensuring that plans and assignments are carried out, and for seeing that the club fulfils its obligations to the Association. The Chairperson should be familiar with the policies and procedures of the club, with the club’s bylaws, and with the duties of all the other club officers and committee members. The newly elected Chairperson should schedule an executive committee meeting which includes the outgoing officers as well. At that time, the Chairperson required to schedule executive council meetings for the entire year. Keep in mind the advantages of scheduling the meeting at the same time each month. Before getting too far along in the club’s year, the Chairperson should do the following: appoint or establish nominating committee according to bylaws for next year’s elections; communicate activities of the club to the faculty mentor; set audit procedures in motion at year end for financial records turnover; and plan a budget with the club Treasurer,

to schedule executive council meetings for the entire year. Keep in mind the advantages of scheduling the meeting at the same time each month. Before getting too far along in the club’s year, the Chairperson should do the following: appoint or establish nominating committee according to bylaws for next year’s elections; communicate activities of the club to the faculty mentor; set audit procedures in motion at year end for financial records turnover; and plan a budget with the club Treasurer, to be reviewed and approved by the entire core committee. One of the most important duties of the club Chairperson is finding enthusiastic and qualified members to serve as executive committee members. The Chairperson should strive to bring new students from first year into the committee structure to provide training for future club leaders. It is strongly recommended that the club chairperson take the time at general meetings to acknowledge those club volunteers whose help has been valuable. This positive feedback and public recognition of good works is often just the encouragement an active volunteer needs to commit to a longer-term leadership position in the club in future years. --- PAGE 11 --- The Club Vice Chair The club Vice Chairperson should be prepared to assume the office of Chairperson or temporarily act in that position at any time, assist the Chair in the management of the Club, and perform other duties that may be assigned by the Chair. The Vice Chairperson should be familiar with the duties and responsibilities of the Chairperson, and of the other elected officers and committee chairpersons. The Vice Chairperson can be an integral component of the club’s operation. In most cases, the Vice Chairperson is responsible for overseeing the activities and progress of the various committee and club programs. The Club Treasurer The club’s financial management and operation are of great importance to the club. The club Treasurer is responsible for the fiscal operation and reporting of the club, and of all committees and programs established by the club. The ongoing responsibilities of the club treasurer include: 1. Maintaining all club financial records. 2. Filing mandatory annual financial report with School. 3. Reporting financial status and requirements to club. 4. Contacting sponsors for financial aid in different event organized by the club. The Club Secretary The ongoing responsibilities of the Club Secretary include: 1. Informing faculty mentor of the names and contact information of

and reporting of the club, and of all committees and programs established by the club. The ongoing responsibilities of the club treasurer include: 1. Maintaining all club financial records. 2. Filing mandatory annual financial report with School. 3. Reporting financial status and requirements to club. 4. Contacting sponsors for financial aid in different event organized by the club. The Club Secretary The ongoing responsibilities of the Club Secretary include: 1. Informing faculty mentor of the names and contact information of new officers and providing School with updated membership lists. 2. Sending agenda to committee members for executive meetings and recording the minutes of such meetings. 3. Handling all club correspondence on a timely basis. 4. Maintaining club files for historical purposes. The Club Marketing & PR Secretary The role of a Student Club Marketing & PR Secretary typically involves managing the club's public relations, marketing strategies, and communication efforts. This person is responsible for promoting the club's activities, events, and brand, ensuring that the club maintains a positive image both within the school and in the wider community. Specific duties might include: Social Media Management: Overseeing the club's social media accounts, posting updates, and engaging with followers. --- PAGE 12 --- Event Promotion: Creating and distributing promotional materials for events, such as posters, flyers, and online content. Communication: Drafting and sending newsletters or emails to club members and other stakeholders. Brand Management: Ensuring that all promotional materials are consistent with the club’s branding guidelines. Public Relations: Managing the club’s relationships with other organizations, sponsors, and the media. The Faculty Mentor The Mentor shall be a faculty member of school. Each Student Club has one Mentor. The faculty mentor shall be generally responsible for the activities of the Club. Specifically, 1. The Mentor helps provide continuity from year to year as student leadership and personnel change. 2. Promotes good student-faculty relationships. 3. Helps maintain University/School standards in all activities of the Club. 4. Exercises financial supervision, if necessary, by promoting prompt payment of bills and collection of dues and overseeing the settlement of all accounts in the event of dissolution of the Club. 4. IDEAS FOR CLUB ACTIVITIES The kinds of activities a club can undertake are endless, depending on the creativity and interest of each group. But here are a few examples of activities that may be of interest to particular club: Distinguished Speakers Program/Lectures: Invite one of the Distinguished Speakers to speak with your club on a topic of your choice. Plan the event with other local organizations and publicize it well to recruit a big audience or even new club members. Panel Discussions and Roundtables: Invite three or four people from the technology workforce or organize the event with members from your club. This will encourage discussions and exchange of ideas and opinions. Joint Meetings: Joint meetings are a great way to interact with local professionals. They are also an opportunity for Professional Clubs to meet with local Student Clubs to teach and advise. Mixers/Social Events: Host an event, dinner, or dessert party where the main purpose is to meet and greet. Encourage others to interact and exchange thoughts and ideas. For a change of pace, these events can be hosted at bowling alleys, bars, restaurants or at a park on a nice day. Sports Teams: Enlist your club in a local sporting league. This will promote unity and bonding amongst club members, as well as encourage friendly competition! Holiday Parties: Celebrate the holidays together with food, drinks, games and prizes! A great chance to get to know all the members and officers better. --- PAGE 13 --- How-to Presentations: Have an expert in a specific field demonstrate and teach. How-to’s can range from anything including “How to fix a resume” to “How to use Photoshop,” and more. The sky is the limit! Fundraising: Fundraising is a great way to earn money for your club to help with winner reward, promotional activities etc. Conferences/ Workshops: Sponsor or co-sponsor a conference/workshop with a local non-profit organization. A conference/workshop is an excellent platform for researchers in all current areas of information technology to present their latest research findings, as well as an open forum for networking and exchanging of ideas. way to earn money for your club to help with winner reward, promotional activities etc. Conferences/ Workshops: Sponsor or co-sponsor a conference/workshop with a local non-profit organization. A conference/workshop is an excellent platform for researchers in all current areas of information technology to present their latest research findings, as well as an open forum for networking and exchanging of ideas. Networking: Interact with other people to exchange information and develop contacts. Seminars: Seminars bring together small groups for recurring meetings, focusing each time on some particular subject, in which everyone present is requested to actively participate. This is often accomplished through an ongoing dialogue with a seminar leader or instructor, or through a more formal presentation of research. They are essentially a place where assigned readings are discussed, questions can be raised and debates can be conducted. They are relatively informal. High School Students Shadowing College Students: Pair high school students with college students. The undergraduate should invite the high school student to spend the day with him or her, attending classes and eating meals together. (High school vacation days work well.) College Students Shadowing Professionals: Pair college students with professionals in the students’ desired career field. A student can spend the day with the professional at his/her place of work. Club Posters: Gather a group of students to make posters advertising all of the schoolevents. Alternately, students may create posters advertising course offerings for the upcoming semester, focusing on the fact that just one computer science course can be beneficial regardless of the person’s major. Poster Session: Organize a small poster session for students who have conducted summer research and participated in internships. Banquet/Award Ceremonies: Reward academically outstanding students by recognizing them at an end-of-semester banquet. Rewards can include gift cards for local restaurants or stores, plaques, or certificates. Be sure to include at least one “most improved” award. Mentoring: Pair younger students with older students based on similar interests and hobbies. Make sure that someone in the group is made explicitly responsible for driving group activities, so momentum begins early and keeps going. Tutoring: If at all possible, find funding for departmental tutors. (In extreme cases, where no funding can be secured, ask student volunteers to tutor their peers in specific classes.) Tutors can sign up for time slots; the information can be emailed to majors; and students can meet the tutors in the computer labs or in

Tutoring: If at all possible, find funding for departmental tutors. (In extreme cases, where no funding can be secured, ask student volunteers to tutor their peers in specific classes.) Tutors can sign up for time slots; the information can be emailed to majors; and students can meet the tutors in the computer labs or in special reserved rooms. Alternatively, your club may want to organize volunteers to tutor local high school students, using a location on the high school campus. Make a Website: Make a website for your club. Study Sessions: For classes, have students from the same classes can meet the night before a test to review the material and ask questions. For GRE or graduate school preparation, ask faculty members --- PAGE 14 --- to hold a study session for students interested in taking the GRE. Afterwards, encourage students to review each subject area (such as computer networks) as a group, using at least two sessions where all students prepare for the first meeting using notes and textbooks from previous classes. Individual members of the group prepare presentations that address unanswered questions in a second meeting. Allowing the students to ask experienced faculty members questions about the Exams will calm nerves and kick off the test preparation phase. “Dividing and conquering” further preparation keeps the group on task and lends efficiency to the process. “Take Apart Your Computer” Day: Faculty members help students explore the inside of a computer. Alternately, student volunteers take computers apart with younger students, such as students from local high schools. Research: Encourage fellow students to submit their research to current Student Research Competition at conferences throughout the year. Bulletin Boards: Gather a group of students to make announcement boards to be placed in public areas, advertising events in the computer science department, career possibilities, research posters, pictures of local award winners and people in-the-news, tutoring opportunities, grants scholarships, other club meetings, etc.. Popular places to post would be restrooms, inside classrooms, and in dorms. Consider fun pictures of officers, blown up to poster size. This also helps to dispel stereotypes. Buddy System: At the beginning of each semester, hold a meeting where people can gather and meet the other people in their classes. The students can then pair up with each other (or form small groups) and set up times to meet outside the classroom throughout the semester, so that the class goes more smoothly for each. Communication Workshop: Find an expert in communication who is willing to volunteer time. Many schools or offices specialize in providing teaching and training for campus groups and departments. Frame the workshop as a general-audience event: How everyone can improve communication practices. Lab Welcoming: The uninviting and unfamiliar COMPUTER SCIENCE laboratory environment can be daunting to people. Gather several older students and invite the laboratory assistants and any other student assistants in the department to a discussion session, providing information that will sensitize the students. The lab assistants should be sure to circulate among the students, be friendly, stop to ask how students who don’t request help are doing, and not wait for students to summon a tutor. Oral Histories: People have valuable stories to share with succeeding “generations” of students. Have people tape each other and edit the footage into stories. Ask each pair to describe how each member chose computing as a major, what struggles s/he has had and how s/he overcame adversity, and her future plans. --- PAGE 15 --- Brain Games: Send out campus flyers from the club. Programming Contests: As a club event you may choose to hold a programming contest for introductory students only, with questions supplied by local professors. The club focuses on building practical skills in cybersecurity through hands-on training, workshops, and competitive events. The Cybersecurity Club collaborates with industry partners such as Wydur Cyber Secure LLC to deliver high-impact learning experiences. Vision and Mission Vision: To continually educate students about cyber awareness and keep members abreast of emerging technologies in cyberspace. The Cybersecurity Club aims to develop into a model club recognized for excellence in cybersecurity education. Mission: • To inculcate cyber ethics and cybersecurity practices among club members • To learn the latest trends and techniques in the cyber world • To improve cyber awareness among rural communities as part of the Digital India initiative • To prepare young minds for careers in the cybersecurity profession Objectives The Cybersecurity Club pursues the following objectives: • Conduct technical training programmes on security tools and methodologies • Help students master security tools through hands-on sessions and competitions • Educate the student community on capabilities to prevent and respond to cyber threats • Help students implement security concepts in academic projects • Conduct cyber awareness programmes for rural populations Activities and Events The Cybersecurity Club organizes the following activities: • Cyber Strike Workshop: A high-impact workshop conducted in collaboration with Wydur Cyber Secure LLC, focusing on practical cybersecurity skills and industry practices • Ethical Hacking Workshops: Regular sessions on penetration testing, vulnerability assessment, and security auditing • Capture The Flag (CTF) Competitions: Internal and inter-college security challenges to test practical skills • Bug Bounty Training: Sessions on identifying and reporting security vulnerabilities --- PAGE 2 --- • Cyber Awareness Campaigns: Outreach programmes for rural communities on digital safety • Burp Suite Masterclass: Expert-led workshops on web application security testing • Network Security Workshops: Training on securing network infrastructure and protocols Membership Details The Cybersecurity

Hacking Workshops: Regular sessions on penetration testing, vulnerability assessment, and security auditing • Capture The Flag (CTF) Competitions: Internal and inter-college security challenges to test practical skills • Bug Bounty Training: Sessions on identifying and reporting security vulnerabilities --- PAGE 2 --- • Cyber Awareness Campaigns: Outreach programmes for rural communities on digital safety • Burp Suite Masterclass: Expert-led workshops on web application security testing • Network Security Workshops: Training on securing network infrastructure and protocols Membership Details The Cybersecurity Club is open to all students of SR University interested in information security. About SR University Source: https://sru.edu.in/about 1.1 History and Background Sri Rajeshwara Educational Society, the parent body of SR University, is a 50-year-old conglomerate of educational institutions with more than 90,000 students and 17,000 teaching and non-teaching staff members. SR Educational Academy, as the governing body, oversees 95 educational institutions spread across the states of Telangana and Andhra Pradesh. SR University was established as the first private university in the state of Telangana. Its campus is situated at Ananthasagar, Hasanparthy, Warangal, Telangana – 506371, India. The EAMCET code for the University is SRHP, and the baseline tuition fee is Rs. 1,30,000 per annum. The goal of SR University is to create an innovative learning educational ecosystem whose graduates significantly contribute to the growth of Telangana and India. The University plans to transform the educational system through three key differentiators: exceptional faculty who engage students through innovative teaching approaches; technology-enabled infrastructure that provides a foundation for entrepreneurial learning through experimentation; and a collaborative entrepreneurial ecosystem that provides curricular, co-curricular, and extra-curricular opportunities for everyone to contribute, thrive, and create a lasting impact. 1.2 Vision and Mission The vision of SR University is to accelerate the pace of transformation and advancement of the regional innovation ecosystem through academic excellence, industry relevance, and social responsibility. The mission of

through three key differentiators: exceptional faculty who engage students through innovative teaching approaches; technology-enabled infrastructure that provides a foundation for entrepreneurial learning through experimentation; and a collaborative entrepreneurial ecosystem that provides curricular, co-curricular, and extra-curricular opportunities for everyone to contribute, thrive, and create a lasting impact. 1.2 Vision and Mission The vision of SR University is to accelerate the pace of transformation and advancement of the regional innovation ecosystem through academic excellence, industry relevance, and social responsibility. The mission of the University is threefold: first, to produce technically competent, industry-ready, and socially conscious leaders; second, to engage in path-breaking research and disseminate the outcomes; and third, to collaborate with industry, government, and non-profit organisations for the benefit of the community. 1.3 Key Differentiators and Ecosystem At the core of SR University, exceptional faculty engage students in new experiences through innovative teaching approaches. SRU is a home for faculty members who are ever curious to question existing knowledge, push the boundaries of understanding through research, and incorporate innovative teaching pedagogies for better learning experiences. Technology-enabled infrastructure provides a foundation for entrepreneurial learning through experimentation, with cutting-edge technology embedded in classrooms and laboratories, helping students familiarise themselves with the latest technological trends in the market. The SRU curriculum fosters an interdisciplinary team-based active learning ecosystem that breaks departmental silos. It creates professionals with a T-shaped personality — individuals who not only have depth of knowledge in one discipline but also have breadth of knowledge across multiple disciplines. Students begin their multidisciplinary journey through a common freshmen experience, interdisciplinary centres, tracks and programs, and interdisciplinary activities. sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 5 --- PAGE 6 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India The five key elements of the SRU ecosystem are as follows. Third, a vibrant learning ecosystem where academic freedom enables faculty to try different teaching techniques and create a productive learning environment. In terms of regional ecosystem engagement, the tri-city area of Warangal-Hanamkonda-Kazipet is a highly developed and economically active part of Telangana. Warangal is positioned to become a significant player in regional and state development, serving as a potential hub for the services sector including IT, ITeS, tourism and hospitality, and textiles and food processing. Key economic movers in the area include IT SEZ, Cyient, Tech Mahindra, Quadrant Resource, Kakatiya Mega Textile Park, and Smart City initiatives. SRU has been an active player in developing the regional ecosystem by engaging regional stakeholders with innovative initiatives, quickly addressing regional needs in farming, textile, and incubation, and working to collaboratively create living-learning regional communities to experiment, learn, and co-create new technologies. 1.4 Rankings and Accreditations SR University holds the 91st rank in the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF) India Rankings. It is ranked 23rd in the UI GreenMetric World University Rankings for sustainability and 50th in THE Impact Rankings for Sustainable Development Goals. SR University holds NBA Tier-1 accreditation for its engineering programs. The University is UGC approved — a copy of the UGC 2F approval is available at https://sru.edu.in/assets/pdf/SR University 2f Approval.PDF. Programs 2026 Source: https://sru.edu.in/programs-2026 3.1 International Engineering Program (IEP) The International Engineering Program (IEP) is offered in two disciplines: Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) and Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE), both in collaboration with top United States universities. The program partner universities are the University of Massachusetts Lowell, the University of New Haven, the University of Missouri, and the University of Central Missouri. The IEP offers flexible study pathways. In the first pathway, a student studies at SR University for the first, second, and third years and then transfers to a partner US university for the fourth year. In the second pathway, the student studies at SR University for the first and second years and transfers to a partner US university for the third and fourth years. An optional fifth year is available for students who wish to pursue a Master of Science (MS) degree from a top US university. To be eligible for the IEP, a candidate must obtain at least 70 percent marks in the subjects specified at the 10+2 level, must have studied Physics, Chemistry, and Mathematics (PCM), and must qualify in the SRSAT, JEE-Main, a state-level engineering entrance exam such as EAPCET or EAMCET, or demonstrate merit in Sports or Cultural Activities. 3.2 Bachelor of Science Advanced Honours — University of Melbourne SR University has a formal collaborative program with the University of Melbourne, Australia, offering the Bachelor of Science Advanced (Honours) degree with CRICOS code

at least 70 percent marks in the subjects specified at the 10+2 level, must have studied Physics, Chemistry, and Mathematics (PCM), and must qualify in the SRSAT, JEE-Main, a state-level engineering entrance exam such as EAPCET or EAMCET, or demonstrate merit in Sports or Cultural Activities. 3.2 Bachelor of Science Advanced Honours — University of Melbourne SR University has a formal collaborative program with the University of Melbourne, Australia, offering the Bachelor of Science Advanced (Honours) degree with CRICOS code 112780B. This is a four-year program in which students complete the first two years at the SR University campus in Warangal, India, and the final two years at the University of Melbourne's Parkville campus in Melbourne, Australia. During the first two years at SR University, students complete compulsory foundation studies co-designed by the University of Melbourne and SR University. This covers the four major disciplines of science: mathematics, physics, chemistry, and biology. SR University provides all lectures, tutorials, practical classes, and on-site support during these two years and conducts ongoing assessments such as practical reports and essays. SR University awards a Diploma in B.Sc. after the second year. In the third and fourth years at the University of Melbourne, students select a major from those offered in the Bachelor of Science Advanced (Honours) program and complete a minor research thesis in the final year. The University of Melbourne designs and monitors the curriculum, monitors learning outcomes and graduate attributes, and awards the full degree upon completion. The fee structure for this program is as follows. For Years 1 and 2 at SR University, the tuition fee is Rs. 2,50,000 per year and the hostel fee is Rs. 1,00,000 per year. For Years 3 and 4 at the University of Melbourne, fee details are available on the University of Melbourne website at https://study.unimelb.edu.au. To be eligible for entry into SR University for Years 1 and 2, a candidate must have completed 10+2 in any science stream, preferably with mathematics but not mandatorily, with a minimum of 70 percent marks. Students can choose subjects from a diverse range of majors at the University of Melbourne including Agricultural Science, Animal Health and Disease, Animal Science and Management, Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Cell and Developmental Biology, Chemistry, Climate and Weather, Ecosystem Science, Food Science, Geography, Immunology, Infection and Immunity, Microbiology, Neuroscience, Pharmacology, Physics, Plant Science, Genetics, Zoology, and Data Science. 3.3 International Business Accelerated Program (IBAP) The International Business Accelerated Program (IBAP) is a dual-degree 3+2 program combining a Bachelor of Business Administration (BBA) from SR University with an MBA or an MS in Finance or Business Analytics from the University of Massachusetts Lowell, USA. Students study at SR University for the first, second, and third years to complete the BBA degree and then transfer to UMass Lowell for the fourth and fifth years to complete the MBA or MS degree. To be eligible, candidates must obtain at least 60 percent marks at the 10+2 level and must qualify in the SRSAT, JEE-Main, EAPCET, or equivalent state-level entrance exams, or demonstrate merit in Sports or Cultural Activities. 3.4 Accelerated Masters Programs SR University offers several Accelerated Masters programs in partnership with international universities. The first is the Accelerated MS in Computer Systems Technology, in which students study B.Tech at SR University for 3.5 years and then transfer to Louisiana State University Shreveport (LSUS) in the USA for 1.5 years. This program is open to all B.Tech students at SRU who have a minimum CGPA of 6.5, a TOEFL score of 61 or an IELTS score of 6, and no backlogs. The second Accelerated Masters program is the Masters in International Agribusiness Management, offered in collaboration with the International School of Agri Management (ISAM), Spain. Students complete one year of study at ISAM in Spain. This program is open to Agriculture students with at least 65 percent marks, and language requirements are assessed on a case-by-case basis. Students complete one year of study at ISAM in Spain. This program is open to Agriculture students with at least 65 percent marks, and language requirements are assessed on a case-by-case basis. The third set of Accelerated Masters programs covers MS in Finance, MS in Public Administration, and a Professional MBA, all offered in collaboration with the Bloch School of Management at the University of Missouri-Kansas City (UMKC), USA. Students study at SR University for 2.5 years and transfer to UMKC for the remaining 1.5 years. These programs are open to BBA students with at least 65 percent marks. The fourth Accelerated Masters program is the MS in Computer Science, offered in collaboration with the University of Florida, USA. Students complete 3.5 years of B.Tech at SR University and transfer to the University of Florida for 1.5 years. Eligibility requires a minimum CGPA of 7.5, a TOEFL score of 80, and a GRE score of 305. This program is exclusively open to B.Tech CSE students at SRU. In addition, SR University maintains a research collaboration with Missouri University of Science and Technology (Missouri S&T;), USA, which is open to all engineering scholars. sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 10 --- PAGE 11 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India 3.5 Bachelor's Degree Programs SR University offers a wide range of four-year B.Tech programs through its School of Computer Science and AI and School of Engineering. The full details of this program including the curriculum, eligibility, fee structure, and available majors are provided in Section 3.2 of this handbook. Minors and Honours Programs Source: https://sru.edu.in/minors_honours 10.1 Minor Degree Overview Unlike conventional universities, SR University offers every undergraduate student the opportunity to earn a Minor Degree by completing 16 additional credits from a discipline different from their primary major. The Minor Degree allows students to pursue subjects that interest them on a personal level, even if those subjects are unrelated to their primary program of study. Minors can be combined across completely different fields — for example, a student majoring in Computer Science can minor in UI/UX Design, or a student majoring in Civil Engineering can minor in Entrepreneurship. The structural flexibility of the Minor program at SR University is designed to match student intellectual curiosity and to open opportunities for interdisciplinary postgraduate programs and better placement outcomes. 10.2 Eligibility Rules and Process A student is eligible for a UG degree with a Minor subject to the following conditions. For B.Tech students, the candidate must have a CGPA of 7.0 or higher at the end of the fourth semester to register for the minor program and must maintain a CGPA of 7.0 at the time of degree award. The student must successfully earn a minimum of 16 additional credits by registering in courses offered by another department. Open electives from the student's existing curriculum can be counted towards the minor as well. The additional credits must be acquired by overloading during a regular semester or a summer semester.

of 7.0 or higher at the end of the fourth semester to register for the minor program and must maintain a CGPA of 7.0 at the time of degree award. The student must successfully earn a minimum of 16 additional credits by registering in courses offered by another department. Open electives from the student's existing curriculum can be counted towards the minor as well. The additional credits must be acquired by overloading during a regular semester or a summer semester. A minor course that is already listed in the student's primary curriculum as a program core course cannot be opted for as a minor course, since all minor courses must be earned as additional credits outside the primary curriculum. A minimum GPA of 5.0 must be earned in all courses undertaken for the minor. The grades obtained in courses credited towards the minor award are not counted and shall have no influence on the GPA or CGPA of the primary program. If a student fails to meet the CGPA requirement or withdraws from the minor at any point after registration, the student will be dropped from the list of candidates eligible for degree with minor and will receive only the primary degree. However, the additional courses completed will be mentioned in the grade sheet. In addition to all eligibility requirements, a minor must be selected outside the major department. Minors made up exclusively of courses from a single department cannot be earned by students majoring in that department. For example, a student majoring in Business Administration cannot minor in Marketing, Finance, or any other minor from the School of Business. A student must declare the option to pursue a minor in the SRAaP portal before the beginning of the third year of enrolment in the case of B.Tech students, and before the beginning of the second year in the case of BBA, IMBA, and B.Sc. (Hons.) Agriculture students. The Dean Academics Office announces those who are admitted. A student may be admitted to only one minor program at a time. Only one minor program may be pursued simultaneously with the major degree program. 10.3 Fee Structure for Minors sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 22 --- PAGE 23 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India Each student admitted to a Minor for the academic year must pay a fee of Rs. 20,000 in addition to the

Only one minor program may be pursued simultaneously with the major degree program. 10.3 Fee Structure for Minors sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 22 --- PAGE 23 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India Each student admitted to a Minor for the academic year must pay a fee of Rs. 20,000 in addition to the fees for the semesters enrolled in. This fee is payable in two installments: Rs. 10,000 at the time of admission to the minor program, and Rs. 10,000 on completion of two courses prescribed for the minor. Students with a CGPA of 7.5 and above are eligible for a merit scholarship of Rs. 5,000, reducing the first installment to Rs. 7,500 and the second installment to Rs. 7,500 — resulting in a total effective fee of Rs. 15,000. 10.4 Honours Program The Honours program at SR University allows students to pursue an advanced specialisation and deeper academic engagement within their primary discipline. Honours courses are prescribed by each respective school or department. The Honours designation is recorded on the degree and transcript. Specific credit requirements and eligibility details are announced by the respective departments at the time of program registration. For further details, students are advised to consult the department advisor. 10.5 Branch Change Policy SR University offers branch change options for B.Tech students after the completion of the first year. The branch change is subject to the student's academic performance, seat availability in the desired program, and the University's internal policy criteria. This provision allows students who wish to change their primary discipline after experiencing the first year of study to make that transition within the University. Detailed procedures and eligibility norms are communicated by the Dean Academics Office at the appropriate time. sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 23 --- PAGE 24 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India 11. SRiX — SR Innovation Exchange Source: https://www.srix.in 11.1 Overview and Mission SRiX, or SR Innovation Exchange, is the Technology Business Incubator (TBI) of SR University, located within the university campus at Warangal, Telangana. It is a Department of Science and Technology (DST) TBI, meaning it operates under the recognition and support of the Government of India's DST. SRiX is an open incubator that welcomes entrepreneurs from across the country and is not restricted to members of the SR University community. SRiX nurtures startup ideas and technology innovations from the idea stage, provides the resources required to build products and find customer acceptance, and supports startups to gear for growth through access to global partners and investors. The incubator brings entrepreneurs, mentors, researchers, and academicians together to create an inspiring ecosystem that transforms ideas into actions. The deep interaction between the theory and practice of entrepreneurship at SRiX creates unique synergies and is intended to serve as an active catalyst for the growth of Telangana's startup ecosystem. 11.2 Impact and Outcomes As of the time of compiling this document, SRiX has supported over 171 startups, created over 741 jobs, and funded 103 startups with approximately Rs. 13.57 Crore directed towards ideation, prototyping, and seed-stage support. The startups funded and supported by SRiX have collectively raised over Rs. 76 Crore from venture capital firms and angel investors. The international admissions page of SR University also reports related figures of 170 plus startups, 430 plus jobs, over Rs. 36 Crore raised, over Rs. 7.2 Crore disbursed, 25 plus incubated startups, 150 plus internships facilitated, and 800 plus innovators impacted. 11.3 Funding Programs at SRiX SRiX administers several funding programs to support innovators and entrepreneurs at various stages. The NIDHI PRAYAS program provides proof-of-concept funding for early-stage innovators who have a technology idea they wish to validate. The NIDHI Seed Support System (NIDHI SSS) offers seed-stage funding for startups that have validated their concept and are ready for product development and market entry. The NIDHI EiR or Entrepreneur in Residence program supports experienced entrepreneurs who wish to work within the incubator environment and contribute to its ecosystem. The NIDHI Accelerator program offers structured accelerator support. The TIDE 2.0 program, funded by the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), provides support for technology-based entrepreneurship. The Startup India Seed Fund

Seed Support System (NIDHI SSS) offers seed-stage funding for startups that have validated their concept and are ready for product development and market entry. The NIDHI EiR or Entrepreneur in Residence program supports experienced entrepreneurs who wish to work within the incubator environment and contribute to its ecosystem. The NIDHI Accelerator program offers structured accelerator support. The TIDE 2.0 program, funded by the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), provides support for technology-based entrepreneurship. The Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) provides seed funding to early-stage startups. SRiX also facilitates connections with Angel investors and Venture Capital (VC) firms for startups that are ready for equity-based investment. 11.4 Incubation Programs SRiX operates four structured incubation and pre-incubation programs. The Pre-Incubation Program provides structured support for individuals and teams at the ideation stage, helping them validate their ideas sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 24 --- PAGE 25 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India and prepare for full incubation. The InnovationX program (formerly referenced as Pre-InnovationX) is an intensive innovation and product development initiative that guides participants through ideation, prototyping, and go-to-market strategies. The SPRINT program is a fast-track accelerator designed for high-potential startups that need concentrated support over a short period. The Women Entrepreneurship Program is a dedicated initiative that provides targeted support and mentorship for women-led startups and aspiring women entrepreneurs. 11.5 Infrastructure at SRiX The SRiX facility is housed within the SR University campus and provides a full range of infrastructure for startups and innovators. The facility includes an IoT Laboratory equipped for hardware and connected device prototyping. An Incubation Zone with private cabins is provided for incubated startups to operate in a professional environment. A Makers Zone offers hardware fabrication and making facilities. A Prototyping Zone is available for building and testing physical and digital prototypes. A Seminar Hall is available for workshops, investor pitches, and knowledge-sharing events. A Cafeteria provides day-to-day amenity support for residents and visitors of the incubator. sru.edu.in | info@sru.edu.in | 0870-281-8333 Page 25 --- PAGE 26 --- SR UNIVERSITY — OFFICIAL INSTITUTIONAL HANDBOOK 2026 Warangal, Telangana, India 12. Over the past six months, we have witnessed significant improvements in our academic standards, placement opportunities, and research outputs—testaments to the collective efforts of our dedicated faculty and enthusiastic student body. Our student clubs have shown remarkable growth, conducting numerous workshops, coding challenges, and community outreach programs. Through these initiatives, our students have not only honed their technical skills but also fostered teamwork, leadership, and problem-solving abilities—all critical traits in the rapidly evolving tech landscape. Our curricula remain dynamic, blending theoretical foundations with practical exposure. We continue to refine our programs in collaboration with industry experts, ensuring our graduates are competitive and well-prepared for the challenges of the modern corporate environment. In the last half-year, more students have secured interviews and offers from leading companies. --- PAGE 3 --- SR UNIVERSITY Dr. Sheshikala Martha Head Of Department The School of Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence has recorded consistent improvement in its Academic, Placement and Research performances. It offers a range of innovatively designed scheme, whose curricula is constantly updated to meet the ever changing requirement of the corporates and to meet the expectations of major stakeholders. We have designed our syllabus to strike a balance between professional knowledge and personal skills. We are confident that our current curriculum has enabled the overall development of blooming professionals to meet the expectations of the corporate and research world. During study at the department, the students are encouraged to get hands-on experience in the potential areas desired by corporate world and research organizations through innovative pedagogical methods ensuring participation of all types of learners with supporting state of the

We have designed our syllabus to strike a balance between professional knowledge and personal skills. We are confident that our current curriculum has enabled the overall development of blooming professionals to meet the expectations of the corporate and research world. During study at the department, the students are encouraged to get hands-on experience in the potential areas desired by corporate world and research organizations through innovative pedagogical methods ensuring participation of all types of learners with supporting state of the art infrastructure. The majority of our graduates were hired by well-known companies both in India and elsewhere. Every year, a sizable number of students apply to prominent universities all over the world. Our department works extremely hard to uphold the ideals upon which it was established. I firmly feel that our students have continually outperformed expectations of research organizations and the business world, fitting in seamlessly with their job profiles. With all of these factors, one may conclude that our students are truthful, smart, diligent, practical, and effective in any work situation. We are happy to see numerous firms interested in visiting our department for Industry backed projects and campus recruitment, as this reinforces our trust in the success of our curriculum and its readiness to meet the dynamic corporate world. With this quick introduction, I would like to invite you to join us on our mission to become a world-class center of excellence in teaching, training, and research. --- PAGE 4 --- SR UNIVERSITY Introduction to the School History SREC was established in 2002 and is sponsored by the SR Educational Society. The institution has been ranked Number 91 in NIRF Engineering ranking category and 1st rank in self-financed private institutions in India by All India ranking for institutions innovation by MHRD. SR Engineering College has been given the status of private University by the State of Telangana and then named SR University. Campus The SR University campus is located in Ananthasagar village of Hasanparthy Mandal near Hanamkonda, Telangana, India. It is in 150 acres, with both separate hostel facilities for boys and girls. There is a huge central library along with Indias largest Technology Business Incubator (TBI) in tier 2 cities. They act as the voice of their peers, ensuring that students’ opinions, concerns, and suggestions are communicated clearly to the faculty and administration. By addressing academic and administrative issues promptly, batch representatives contribute significantly to creating a supportive and inclusive learning environment. Beyond communication, they play a key part in fostering unity within the batch. They coordinate between different student groups, help in conflict resolution, and ensure that every student feels heard and valued. Their presence is essential in bridging gaps, whether it's clarifying doubts about coursework, representing collective feedback, or facilitating participation in institutional events and decision-making Improving and Demonstrating Leadership Skills Being a batch representative also provides a platform to strengthen one’s leadership abilities. One of the most important traits is active listening—t. ruly understanding the needs and concerns of fellow students before acting on them. This builds trust and respect. Additionally, adaptability is critical in addressing the wide range of challenges that may arise, whether related to academic pressures, communication gaps, or interpersonal issues within the group. Furthermore, effective representatives foster a collaborative and motivating atmosphere. They lead by example, inspire teamwork, and encourage students to engage actively in academic and extracurricular activities. By promoting inclusiveness, empathy, and cooperation, they empower others to contribute meaningfully to the collective success of the batch. In essence, batch representatives not only help in enhancing the academic experience of their peers but also grow personally and professionally. The role nurtures critical leadership qualities such as responsibility, initiative, problem-solving, and emotional intelligence—traits that are invaluable both in academic life and beyond. --- PAGE 23 --- SR UNIVERSITY School Achievements SR Engineering College has been honored with the Best Performing Academy award by the Cisco Networking Academy in collaboration with TASK. Student Handbook --- PAGE 14 --- LEADERSHIP AT SRU Chancellor Sri A. Varada Reddy is a pioneer in providing quality education and his efforts have transformed the lives of many youngsters. A man known for discipline and vision; Sri Varada Reddy has been serving the field of education since 1976. With his unstinted efforts, SR has become a trusted brand name in Telangana. Under his leadership, 185 educational institutions, including schools, junior colleges, degree colleges and professional colleges across Telangana, Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka are fostering excellent standards of education to young minds, shaping them into intellectuals. Sri. Varada Reddy has defined quality consciousness coupled with ethical practices as the DNA of SR group. Vice-Chancellor Prof. Deepak Garg is PhD in Computer Science with specialization in Efficient Algorithm Design. With 25 years of experience, he has worked as a Professor and Dean, School of Computer Science at Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology, Patiala and Bennett University, Greater Noida. He is a Distinguished Professor in Artificial Intelligence and known as one of the top voices in the country on AI. He is a regular Blogger in the Times of India with a nickname of “Breaking Shackles” and writes about Higher Education landscape and AI. He is on the Advisory Committee of AIRAWAT, Super Computing Mission of Govt of India to create a 1 Exaflop backbone infrastructure for the country. He has represented on various Committees at NAAC, NBA, UGC and AICTE. Working with Startup Founders is his passion, and he serves on the advisory board of Drishya AI, ByteXL and Global AI Hub. For extensive bus route information, please visit TSRTC (telangana.gov.in). 2.4 Warangal and its History SRU is situated in Warangal ‘’ORUGALLU’’ or Ekashila Nagar, the name was given to the capital of the Kakatiya empire during that time. The fort was constructed with a single massive stone, hence the later designation of “Warangal.” Pakhal Lake was built by the famous Kakatiya, Telugu ruler Ganapati Deva in the densest part of the forest to be used for farming in the future. About 3,000,000 acres are now being submerged by it. The Kakatiya kings had many plans in the works to improve people’s lives in the future. They employed a wide range of engineering techniques; for example, the Ramappa temple was built on a lake, and the thousand pillar temples of Hanamkonda were built on the sandy ground; both have survived for a millennium. The Kakatiya dynasty, which ruled from 1163 to 1565, had its capital in Warangal. Fortresses, lakes, temples, and stone gates are only some of the Kakatiya dynasty’s contributions to the city’s status as a tourist hotspot. National Highway 163 links Hyderabad, Bhuvanagiri, Warangal, and Bhopalpatnam. Kazipet Junction and Warangal are two of the most important stops. Warangal is well connected to the metro city Hyderabad which is 180 km from the main city and well connected by rail and road. City is full of shopping areas, eateries, restaurants, and other places of entertainment. There are lot of schools for good educations of your children from primary to K12 level. City is also known as a medical hub and has ample facilities for any health- related issues. It is a dynamic and inclusive community of students passionate about AI, Machine Learning, and its applications in Electrical Engineering. This club aims to provide a platform for students to learn, collaborate, and innovate in the rapidly evolving field of AI applications in Electrical Engineering. Simulation Club The Simulation Club is a dynamic and innovative organization which serves as a platform for students, enthusiasts, and professionals alike, who share a common passion for simulation technologies and their application in the field of electrical engineering. The club’s primary mission is to foster a vibrant community that promotes hands-on learning, creativity, and collaboration through simulation-based projects. By utilizing cutting-edge software and tools, the club aims to deepen participants’ understanding of complex electrical systems and enhance problem-solving skills. Electrical Engineering The Electrical Engineering Association (EEA) of SR University Association (EEA) serves as a place of community for students to discuss, learn and develop technical and professional skills among the electrical engineering students. Members of the EEA are offered with a range of programs and activities such as seminars, workshops, technical quizzes, poster presentations, certifications, hackathons, project competitions and exposition. The EEA is supported by both internal and external experts who assist the students in strategizing and organizing activities that will help them enhance their knowledge and skills in Electrical Engineering. Student Clubs @ Department of Electronics & Communication Engineering (ECE) Club Name Description Byte Optimizers Club Byte Optimizers strives to encourage interest and knowledge in the Internet of Things (IoT). The club brings collectively students who are excited about emerging technologies, smart devices, and the potential of IoT to transform multiple sectors and daily life. Workshops, skill development, and invited talks will be among the activities offered by this club. Intelligence The Intelligence Ignite club amalgamates the most stimulating aspects of artificial intelligence (AI) and the cutting-edge field of Ignite Club Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE). The club’s goal is to bridge the gap between AI and ECE by creating a collaborative environment in which students with diverse interests can investigate the synergy between these two fields. Intelligence Ignite’s activities will include guest lectures, workshops, and tutorials focusing on integrating AI techniques into ECE applications. 43 SRU Student Handbook

Tech Connectors Club The Tech Connectors Club brings collectively students who are passionate about wireless communication and networking. SRU Policies Conduct on Campus SR University believes in providing students with the freedom and knowledge to support their decisions, and it trusts them to act in their own best interests as well as the interests of the university. The university then anticipates that students will exhibit accountability and self-control. The idea is to instill a sense of ownership so that this conduct becomes second nature. This includes respecting the rights of others to freedom of speech and making appropriate use of the resources at your disposal. Students come from a variety of origins, viewpoints, genders, locations, faiths, cultures, and experiences. The success of the SRU community is greatly influenced by inclusivity. Everyone attending the university is expected to respect and be aware of diversity. The intention is to create a diversified atmosphere which is beneficial to everyone. Students shall dress decently when in public places and are cautioned that in the era of fast internet and high definition cameras, their pictures and videos can fall in wrong hands and create unpleasant consequences. Anti-Ragging Policy Ragging is a criminal and non-bailable offense. Ragging or abetment of ragging in any form is strictly prohibited within the premises of the University, including its hostels, departments, institutions, schools, colleges, constituent units, centers, campus grounds, and any part of the SR University group institutions, as well as on public transport systems. Any violation will be dealt with in accordance with the regulations, directives, and guidelines, including: - Supreme Court Guidelines and - UGC Regulations, 2009. The university has constituted an anti-ragging cell, headed by the Dean (Student Affairs) along with committee members from all schools and hostel wardens. The cell ensures that the freshers are protected from the menace of ragging. The cell deploys faculty in key areas such as hostels, canteen, bus stops etc. to prevent ragging and also ensures that action will be taken against those who indulge in such activities. The students can call the anti-ragging cell at stu.antiraggingcell@sru.edu.in or help desk to register any complaint or can directly reach out to the Office of Student Affairs. Dr. A.V.V. Academic Council is an authority as per University Grants Commission (UGC) regulation and it has the right to take decisions on all academic matters including academic research. Academic Year: This is the period comprising of two regular semesters i.e., Odd Semester and Even Semester. In addition to the regular semesters, there may be summer / winter term. Audit Course: It is a noncredit course of study. Backlog Course: A course is a backlog course if the student has obtained a grade (R / F ) Basic Sciences: The foundation courses in the areas of Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology are offered in this category. Board of Studies: The Board of Studies (BoS) is a body as defined in the UGC regulations, constituted by Vice-chancellor for each of the department separately. The functions of BoS include framing the content of various courses, reviewing, and updating the content from time to time, introducing latest programs etc. Branch of Study: An area of study or a specified program (like Computer Science Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, Electrical and Electronics Engineering etc.). Capstone / Industry Project: Course that a student must undergo during final year which involves the student to undertake a research, startup or design, which is carefully planned to achieve a particular aim. It is a credit-based course. Career Readiness Elective is a course that focuses on developing students’ verbal and aptitude skills to enhance their preparedness for future careers. Certificate Course: This course makes a student gain firsthand expertise and skills required for development in each area. Change of Branch: Transfer from one’s branch of study to the other branch (e.g., change from B.Tech. Computer Science Engineering to B.Tech. Mechanical Engineering). Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE): Continuous assessments used to evaluate student learning, acquired skills, and academic attainment during a course. Course: A course is a subject offered by the University for learning in a particular semester. Course Code: Each course is identified by a unique course code depicting the department / school

for development in each area. Change of Branch: Transfer from one’s branch of study to the other branch (e.g., change from B.Tech. Computer Science Engineering to B.Tech. Mechanical Engineering). Credit: Every course has a certain number of credits assigned to it depending upon the nature and importance of the course. The number of ‘Contact Hours’ in a week of a particular course determines its credit value. One credit is equivalent to one lecture/tutorial hour per week or two hours per week of self- learning/ practical/ project/ field work during a semester. Credit Transfer: The procedure of granting credit(s) to a student for course(s) undertaken at another institution/ University. Cumulative Grade Point Average (CGPA): It is a measure of the cumulative performance of a student over all the completed semesters. The CGPA is the ratio of total credit points secured by a student in various courses in all semesters and the sum of the total credits of all courses in all the semesters. It is expressed up to two decimal places. Curriculum: Curriculum is broadly defined as the totality of student experiences that occur in the educational process. It incorporates the planned sequence of instruction, instructional content, materials, resources, and processes for evaluating the attainment of Program Educational Objectives. Degree: A student who fulfills all the program requirements is eligible to receive a degree. Degree with Specialization: A student who fulfills the entire program requirements of the discipline and successfully completes a specified set of professional elective courses in a specialized area is eligible to receive a degree with specialization. Department: An academic entity that conducts relevant curricular and co-curricular activities, involving both teaching and non-teaching staff and other resources. Detention in a course: Student who does not obtain minimum prescribed attendance in a course shall be detained in that course. Dropping from the Semester: A student who does not want to register for the semester should

Dropping from the Semester: A student who does not want to register for the semester should do so in writing before commencement of the semester. Dual Degree: A dual degree program means that a student can pursue two different degrees from either the same university or two different universities or even from two different countries but at the same time. Elective Course: A course that can be chosen from a set of courses. An elective can be Program Elective, Open Elective and Specialization Elective. Engineering Sciences: The courses belonging to basic evolutionary aspects of engineering from Mechanical Sciences, Electrical Sciences, Civil, Computing etc. Evaluation: Evaluation is the process of judging the academic work done by the student in the courses. It is done through a combination of continuous in-semester assessment and end semester examinations. Grade: It is an index of the performance of the students in a said course. Grades are denoted by alphabets like A, B, C, D etc. In case of School of Agriculture, the grades are denoted by Division I, II etc. 53 SRU Student Handbook --- PAGE 54 --- Grade Point: It is a numerical weight allotted to each letter grade on a 10 - point scale. Hobbies, co-curricular, and extra-curricular elective course is refer to activities that students partici-pate. These activities may include sports, clubs, music, theater, and more. Participation in these activi-ties can help students develop new skills, build social connections, and enhance their overall personal growth and development. Honours Degree: A student who fulfills all the program requirements of the discipline and successfully completes a specified set of additional courses within the same program is eligible to receive an Honors degree. Liberal Arts, Humanities, and Social Sciences Elective is course in the fields of liberal arts, humanities, and social sciences. Minor: A student who fulfills all the program requirements of the discipline and successfully completes a specified set of courses from another discipline is eligible to receive a minor in other discipline. Overall Grade Point Average (OGPA): It is the measure of the total credit points secured by a student for all semesters divided by total credits of the courses registered and rounded off to three decimals. Open Elective: This is a course of interdisciplinary nature. It is offered across all engineering branches of University. Overloading: Registering for a greater number of credits than normally prescribed by the Program in a semester. Practice School: It is a part of the total program and takes one full semester in a professional location, where the students and the faculty get involved in finding solutions to real world problems. A student can choose Project/Practice School during 7th or 8th semester of academic Year to meet the final requirements for a degree. Pre-requisite: A course, the knowledge of which is required for registration into higher level course. Program Educational Objectives (PEOs): Program Educational Objectives are broad statements that describe the career and professional accomplishments that the program is preparing graduates to achieve. Program Outcomes (POs): Program Outcomes are statements that describe what students are expected to know and be able to do upon graduating from the program. These relate to the skills, knowledge, attitude, and behaviour that students acquire through the program. Program Specific Outcomes (PSOs): PSOs are a statement that describes what students are expected to know and be able to do in a specialized area of discipline upon graduation from a program. In a Program there may be 2-4 PSOs, if required. These are the statements, which are specific to the program. They are beyond POs. Program Curriculum and other activities during the program must help in the achievement of PSOs along with POs. Registration: Process of student enrollment into a set of course(s) in a semester/summer /winter semester of the Program. Re-Registering: A student who wishes to repeat a course is permitted to do so, subject to the regulations contained herein. Semester: It is a period of study comprising of 14 to 18 weeks of academic/ research engagement equivalent to 90 working days including the examination. In case of programs offered by School of Agriculture, semester comprises of 110 working days including examinations. End Semester Examinations (ESE): It is an examination conducted at the end of the course of study every semester.. Seminar on Emerging Technologies: A mandatory credit course that a student should undergo to enhance his/her knowledge on emerging technologies. Specialization Core: Focused courses within an academic program that provide in-depth knowledge and skills in a specific area of study is known as specialization core Specialization Elective: An elective course offered by a Department/ School for the fulfillment of degree with specialization is known as specialization elective.

Degree with Honours A student is eligible for a Degree with Honours, subject to the following: i. Student should have a CGPA of 7.0 or higher at the time of start of the honors courses in the curriculum to register for honours program and maintain a CGPA of 7.0 at the time of award of degree. ii. Student must earn a minimum of 16 additional credits through advanced courses other than the courses required as per the program by registering for those courses. iii. Student must acquire the additional credits by overloading during a regular semester or summer / winter semester. iv. In case, a student fails to meet the CGPA requirement at the time of award of a degree or withdraws for a degree with honours at any point after registration, student will be dropped from the list of students eligible for a degree with honours and they will receive their major degree only. However, the additional courses completed by them will be mentioned in their grade sheet. v. Students must pay extra fees for all the courses registered for honours. vi. The grades obtained in the courses credited towards the honours award are not counted and shall have no influence on the SGPA/ CGPA of the ‘program’ in which the student has enrolled in the university. In these customized programs, students seamlessly transfer to the partner university during their final year (7th or 8th Semester) to obtain a bachelor’s degree from SRU and a master’s degree from the partner institution. The distinct advantage lies in students com-pleting their programs ahead of their peers, leading to both time and financial savings. Notably, these programs often waive GRE/TOEFL requirements, streamlining the application process. The beauty of this arrangement extends to post-study work rights, as participating in these Master’s Programs does not compromise the ability to pursue employment in the host country. This innovative approach not only accelerates academic achievement but also enhances the overall value of the educational experience for SRU students. Attendance Policy A student is expected to maintain 100% attendance in all courses. However, because a student may need leave due to ill-health or to attend some family emergency, a student is permitted to maintain a minimum of 75% attendance (i.e., absent for 25% of instructional hours) in each course without producing any proof for the absence. This 25% absence includes medical, personnel, casual, official leave of absence for organizing events/ seminars/ workshops/ technical/ cultural festivals/competitions/ participation in co- curricular/ extra- curricular events/ NSS & NCC camps, or any other (valid or otherwise) reason. In case of medical exigencies, the student/parent should inform the concerned instructor/Dept immediately by official email/other means of communication. • In case of attendance falls marginally below 75% due to severe medical reasons or any other valid reasons, the School Dean may bring such cases, along with valid and adequate evidence, to the notice of the Dean (Academics). • The condonation board led by Dean (Academics) will consider any further relaxation in attendance from the minimum 75% condition after going through case by case. 65 SRU Student Handbook --- PAGE 66 --- • A 10-25% waiver is at the discretion of the condonation board, in which case there shall be a financial penalty levied as condonation fees in proportion to the charges are mentioned in the Annexure-I. • In case of students belonging to School of Agriculture, only a 10% waiver is at the discretion of the condonation board, in which case there shall be a financial penalty levied as condonation fees in proportion to the Condonation. Absence in Assessments and Mid-Term Examination If a student fails to take the Mid Term Examination or obtains less than 50% marks in the Mid Term Examination, then student may be given a makeup exam option. F grade: This grade is awarded to undergraduate (UG) students who have attendance more than 75% in a course but fail to achieve a minimum of 40% in the overall semester evaluation including continuous assessments and end exams. For postgraduate (PG) students, this grade is given when they maintain attendance of over 75% but fail to achieve a minimum of 50% in the overall semester evaluation including continuous assessments and end exams. R grade: If a student is detained based on attendance or due to any penalty or drops a semester, ‘R’ grade is awarded. A student, who earns ‘R’ grade in a course, shall register for that course in summer/winter semester. I grade: If a student fails to appear for the end semester examination due to medical reasons, ‘I’ grade will be awarded. CGPA is the weighted average of all the grades awarded to a student since his entry into the University up to and including the latest semester and is computed as follows. CGPA = (ΣCi Gi) / (ΣCi) where Ci is the number of credits assigned to ith course and Gi is the grade point equivalent to the letter grade obtained by the student in the ith course. When a student repeats a course, the new grade will replace the earlier one in the calculation of the CGPA. While calculating CGPA, I grade secured by the student shall not be considered. Formula for conversion of CGPA to percentage of marks is (10 x CGPA). Dean (Academics) will approve registration of students who have backlog course(s) in each semester. However, the student may be allowed to study an equivalent course (against the backlog

Minimum Academic Requirement for Continuing the Program A student will be allowed to continue in the respective program only if, a.) At the end of first year, student i) Secures a CGPA of greater than or equal to 3.50 OR ii) Earns a minimum of 40% of the credits offered in the approved scheme of courses in the first year. b.) At the end of second year, student i) Secures a CGPA of greater than or equal to 4.00 OR ii) Earns a minimum of 50% of the credits offered in the approved scheme of courses in the first year. c.) At the end of Third year, student i) Secures a CGPA of greater than or equal to 4.00 OR ii) Earns a minimum of 50% of the credits offered in the approved scheme of courses in the second year d.) ** A student who fails to satisfy both the conditions mentioned in the above paras i) and ii) of sub-clause (a) or (b) will be required to repeat the entire year. For the Lateral entry students, clause (b) only will be applicable. Grade Improvement Policy The university understands that students, due to various reasons, at times do not perform to their full potential resulting in lower CGPA than desired. As the overall CGPA and grades remain with students all their life, the university proposes the grade improvement policy so that students can improve their grades. Eligibility & Rules i. Students having grade B to D are eligible for grade improvement in a course. ii. There is no capping of grade and the student can even get an A grade with grade improvement iii. The student can take a maximum of one attempt to upgrade his/her grade for a particular course and any further attempts shall not be allowed. iv. Revised grade shall be considered for eligibility of Honors/Minors Courses, internships/ placements and also international admissions based on CGPA.. v. In case of grade improvement for a course, the better of the two grades is considered. vi. The students shall go through the full course and all components of evaluation, 73 SRU Student Handbook --- PAGE 74 --- 74 SRU Student Handbook --- PAGE 75 --- irrespective of his/her previous participation in the evaluation components of the same course. The grade of the course and evaluation shall be considered completely independent of each other. vii. Student will be required to register for the grade improvement in a course as per the registration schedule notified by the Examination Branch before every Summer semester. viii. Grade improvement shall not be allowed after the student passes out of the university or has been awarded a degree certificate. ix. The revised grade sheet shall specifically be mentioned as “Revised Grade Sheet” to distinguish it from the previous grade sheet released to the student. x. The revised CGPA, calculated after considering the revised grade(s) shall be known as revised CGPA. Student shoud pay additional fee fro credit as per Annexure-I. Policy on Backlog Courses/ Not Registered Courses To facilitate the students to clear their backlogs / not registered courses and enable them to pass out with their batch mates, the following provisions are offered: (a) Backlog courses will be allowed in the Winter / Summer Semester. (b) The previous batches shall be given the option to appear for their backlogs in Summer Term / Winter Term. (c) The number of credits offered for the Winter or Summer semester is contingent upon the availability of Teaching days. Student can take maximum credits offered in winter/summer semester to clear backlog or grade improvement. (d) Student will be required to register for the backlog / not registered courses as per the registration schedule notified by the Examination Branch before every Winter / Summer Semester.
